Izmkk1Ry3Wc.prompt: What is the defining difference between an initial value problem and a boundary value problem?
Izmkk1Ry3Wc.hint: Think about where along the interval the side conditions are imposed.

answerOptions.text: An IVP gives all conditions at one point; a BVP gives conditions at two different points
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. An initial value problem fixes the state at a single point, while a boundary value problem imposes conditions at two endpoints.

answerOptions.text: An IVP is linear and a BVP is nonlinear
answerOptions.rationale: Linearity is unrelated to this distinction; both kinds can be linear. Where are the conditions specified in each?

answerOptions.text: An IVP has no solution but a BVP always does
answerOptions.rationale: Existence is not the dividing line, and BVPs need not have solutions. What about the location of the conditions?

answerOptions.text: An IVP is first order and a BVP is second order
answerOptions.rationale: Order does not separate them; both can be second order. What distinguishes where conditions are placed?

Izmkk1Ry3Wc.prompt: Which side conditions on $y'' + y = 0$ make it a boundary value problem?
Izmkk1Ry3Wc.hint: Look for conditions imposed at two distinct values of the variable.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Conditions at the two separate points $0$ and $\pi$ make this a boundary value problem.
Both of these are imposed at the single point $0$. Does that match an IVP or a BVP?
One condition underdetermines a second-order equation, and it sits at one point. What setup uses two endpoints?
These are both at $0$ and use derivatives at one point. Which option places conditions at two points?

Izmkk1Ry3Wc.prompt: How many side conditions does a second-order linear equation require to single out a particular solution?
Izmkk1Ry3Wc.hint: The number of conditions matches the order of the equation.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A second-order equation has two arbitrary constants, so two conditions are needed.
One condition leaves an arbitrary constant unfixed. How many constants does the general solution carry?
That exceeds the number of arbitrary constants. How many constants does a second-order general solution have?
Without conditions the solution stays general. How many constants must be pinned down?

Izmkk1Ry3Wc.prompt: Compared with initial value problems, what is true of existence and uniqueness for boundary value problems?
Izmkk1Ry3Wc.hint: Recall the IVP guarantee, then ask whether the two-endpoint setup keeps it.

answerOptions.text: A BVP may have no solution, one solution, or infinitely many
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Unlike the IVP guarantee, a boundary value problem can fail to have a solution or can have many.

answerOptions.text: A BVP always has exactly one solution
answerOptions.rationale: The clean IVP uniqueness does not carry over. What range of outcomes is possible at two endpoints?

answerOptions.text: A BVP never has a solution
answerOptions.rationale: Many boundary value problems do have solutions. What set of outcomes is actually possible?

answerOptions.text: A BVP has the same guarantee as an IVP
answerOptions.rationale: The two-endpoint structure breaks the IVP guarantee. What outcomes become possible?

Izmkk1Ry3Wc.prompt: The problem $y'' + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y'(0) = 1$ is which type?
Izmkk1Ry3Wc.hint: Check whether both conditions sit at the same point.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Both conditions are given at the single point $0$, so it is an initial value problem.
A BVP needs conditions at two different points. Are these at one point or two?

answerOptions.text: Neither, since it has two conditions
answerOptions.rationale: Two conditions are exactly right for second order. Where are they placed here?
There is one independent variable, so it is an ODE. Where are its conditions located?

E97SZm2ZrBo.prompt: On an interval $[a, b]$, a typical pair of boundary conditions specifies what?
E97SZm2ZrBo.hint: Boundary conditions live at the ends of the spatial interval.

answerOptions.text: Values (or derivative values) of $y$ at $x = a$ and at $x = b$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Boundary conditions constrain the solution at the two endpoints of the interval.

answerOptions.text: The value and first derivative of $y$ at $x = a$ only
answerOptions.rationale: That is the initial-value pattern at one end. Where does the other condition belong for a BVP?

answerOptions.text: The values of $y$ at infinitely many interior points
answerOptions.rationale: Two conditions suffice for second order, placed at the ends. Which points carry them?

answerOptions.text: The coefficients of the differential equation
answerOptions.rationale: Coefficients define the equation, not the side conditions. What do boundary conditions constrain?

E97SZm2ZrBo.prompt: A Dirichlet boundary condition specifies which quantity at an endpoint?
E97SZm2ZrBo.hint: Dirichlet fixes the function value itself.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A Dirichlet condition prescribes the value of the unknown function at the endpoint.
Prescribing the derivative is a different (Neumann) condition. What does Dirichlet fix directly?
Second derivatives are not what boundary conditions usually set. Which quantity does Dirichlet name?

answerOptions.text: The integral of $y$ over the interval
answerOptions.rationale: An integral constraint is a different kind of condition. What pointwise value does Dirichlet give?

E97SZm2ZrBo.prompt: A Neumann boundary condition specifies which quantity at an endpoint?
E97SZm2ZrBo.hint: Neumann constrains the slope of the solution.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A Neumann condition prescribes the derivative of the function at the endpoint.
Fixing the value itself is the Dirichlet condition. What does Neumann fix instead?
No nonlinear product is involved. Which single derivative does Neumann set?
Boundary conditions normally use $y$ or $y'$. Which one does Neumann name?

E97SZm2ZrBo.prompt: A steady temperature distribution in a rod with both ends held at fixed temperatures is modeled by which kind of problem?
E97SZm2ZrBo.hint: Conditions are imposed at the two ends of the rod.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Fixing the temperature at each end of the rod imposes conditions at two points, a boundary value problem.
The conditions are at the two ends in space, not at a single starting instant. What type is that?
Two fixed end temperatures are two conditions. Where in the domain do they sit?
The model is a differential equation in position. What kind of side conditions does it carry?

E97SZm2ZrBo.prompt: In many boundary value problems the independent variable represents what?
E97SZm2ZrBo.hint: Boundary conditions are tied to the ends of a physical region.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Boundary value problems typically involve a spatial variable with conditions at the edges of the region.
Time-based conditions at one instant give initial value problems. What variable do endpoints usually represent?
The variable is a physical coordinate, not a probability. What does it usually measure?
The variable ranges over an interval with two ends. What does that interval usually represent?

AnyGw-gOY0U.hint: The general solution of $y'' = 0$ is a straight line; fit the two endpoints.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The general solution is $y = c_1 + c_2 x$; $y(0) = 0$ gives $c_1 = 0$ and $y(1) = 2$ gives $c_2 = 2$.
A constant cannot satisfy both $y(0) = 0$ and $y(1) = 2$. What linear function fits both ends?
The general solution of $y'' = 0$ is linear, not quadratic. What line passes through $(0,0)$ and $(1,2)$?
This gives $y(1) = 1$, not $2$. What slope makes $y(1) = 2$ with $y(0) = 0$?

AnyGw-gOY0U.prompt: What is the standard procedure for solving a linear two-point boundary value problem?
AnyGw-gOY0U.hint: Find every solution first, then use the endpoints.

answerOptions.text: Find the general solution, then apply both boundary conditions to fix the constants
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. You build the general solution and then impose the two endpoint conditions to determine the constants.

answerOptions.text: Apply the boundary conditions before solving the equation
answerOptions.rationale: You need the general solution in hand before the constants can be fixed. What comes first?
The conditions are used as given, not differentiated. What do you do with the general solution?

answerOptions.text: Guess the answer and check only one endpoint
answerOptions.rationale: Both endpoints must be satisfied. What systematic procedure uses the general solution?

AnyGw-gOY0U.hint: The general solution is $y = A\cos x + B\sin x$; apply each condition.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. $y(0) = 0$ forces $A = 0$, and $y(\pi/2) = B = 1$, so $y = \sin x$.
Then $y(0) = 1$, violating $y(0) = 0$. Which trig function vanishes at $0$?
The cosine term makes $y(0) = 1 \neq 0$. Which term must drop out?
The cosine piece breaks $y(0) = 0$. What remains after $A = 0$?

AnyGw-gOY0U.prompt: A homogeneous boundary value problem such as $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ always admits which solution?
AnyGw-gOY0U.hint: Ask what happens if the function is identically zero.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The zero function satisfies the equation and both homogeneous boundary conditions for every $\lambda$.

answerOptions.text: A unique nonzero solution for every $\lambda$
answerOptions.rationale: Nonzero solutions appear only for special $\lambda$. What solution works for all $\lambda$?
At least one solution always exists here. Which simple function always fits?
A nonzero constant cannot meet $y(0) = y(L) = 0$. What function does?

AnyGw-gOY0U.prompt: For the homogeneous problem $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$, nontrivial solutions exist only when what holds?
AnyGw-gOY0U.hint: Only special separation values let a nonzero solution meet both ends.

answerOptions.text: $\lambda$ takes one of certain special (eigenvalue) values
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Nonzero solutions occur only at the discrete eigenvalues of the problem.
Most values give only the trivial solution. What kind of values allow nonzero solutions?
Negative $\lambda$ yields only the trivial solution for these conditions. What special values work?
At $\lambda = 0$ the only solution is trivial here. Which discrete values permit nonzero solutions?

8y0IcYqPIgA.prompt: How many boundary conditions does a second-order two-point boundary value problem carry?
8y0IcYqPIgA.hint: The count matches the order of the differential equation.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A second-order equation needs two conditions, here placed one at each end.
One condition underdetermines second order. How many endpoints carry a condition?

answerOptions.text: Three, two at one end and one at the other
answerOptions.rationale: That exceeds the number of constants to fix. How many conditions does second order need?
Only two constants exist to determine. How many conditions are required?

8y0IcYqPIgA.hint: Integrate twice to get $y = x^3 + c_1 x + c_2$, then apply the endpoints.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Integrating gives $y = x^3 + c_1 x + c_2$; $y(0) = 0$ gives $c_2 = 0$ and $y(1) = 1 + c_1 = 1$ gives $c_1 = 0$.
Then $y(1) = 2$, not $1$. What value of $c_1$ makes $y(1) = 1$?
That is $y'$, not the solution of $y'' = 6x$. What do you get after integrating twice?
This violates $y(0) = 0$. What constant term satisfies the left endpoint?

8y0IcYqPIgA.prompt: The problem $y'' + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(\pi) = 0$ has how many solutions?
8y0IcYqPIgA.hint: Apply $y(0) = 0$ to $y = A\cos x + B\sin x$, then test the second condition.

answerOptions.text: Infinitely many, $y = B\sin x$ for any $B$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. $y(0) = 0$ gives $A = 0$, and $y(\pi) = B\sin\pi = 0$ holds for every $B$, so all $B\sin x$ work.
The second condition is satisfied automatically by $\sin x$. What family of nonzero solutions survives?
At least $y \equiv 0$ works, and in fact more do. What does $y(\pi) = B\sin\pi = 0$ allow?
The free constant $B$ is unrestricted here. How many values can $B$ take?

8y0IcYqPIgA.prompt: When a homogeneous two-point problem yields only $y \equiv 0$, the boundary value problem is called what?
8y0IcYqPIgA.hint: Compare with the eigenvalue case where nonzero solutions appear.

answerOptions.text: It has only the trivial solution (the value is not an eigenvalue)
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. If the parameter is not an eigenvalue, the trivial solution is the only one.
Infinitely many appear only at eigenvalues. What is true when only zero solves it?

answerOptions.text: It is an initial value problem
answerOptions.rationale: It is still a boundary value problem. What does getting only $y \equiv 0$ tell you about the parameter?
The zero function is a valid solution. What name fits when it is the only one?

8y0IcYqPIgA.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$, the parameter values giving nonzero solutions are which?
8y0IcYqPIgA.hint: The sine must vanish at $x = L$, forcing $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L$ to be a multiple of $\pi$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $A = 0$, requiring $\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\,L) = 0$ gives $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L = n\pi$, so $\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2$.
The condition $\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\,L) = 0$ involves the square root of $\lambda$. What must you square to solve for $\lambda$?
Solve $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L = n\pi$ for $\lambda$, dividing by $L$ first. What expression results?
That only holds when $L = \pi$. How does the length $L$ enter the formula?

vGwyyXWGR3Y.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$, what are the eigenvalues?
vGwyyXWGR3Y.hint: Nontrivial sines must vanish at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The condition $\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\,L) = 0$ gives $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L = n\pi$, hence $\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2$.
You must square the relation $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L = n\pi$. What is $\lambda$ itself?
Negative values give only the trivial solution here. What sign of $\lambda$ produces oscillating sines?
The interval length $L$ must appear. How does $L$ enter the eigenvalue?

vGwyyXWGR3Y.prompt: The eigenfunctions of $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ are which functions?
vGwyyXWGR3Y.hint: They must vanish at both endpoints.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. These sines vanish at $x = 0$ and $x = L$, matching the boundary conditions.
Cosine does not vanish at $x = 0$. Which function is zero at both ends?
Exponentials do not satisfy these zero boundary conditions. What oscillatory function does?
Powers of $x$ do not vanish at $x = L$ in general. What sine fits both endpoints?

vGwyyXWGR3Y.prompt: For the interval length $L = \pi$, the eigenvalues of $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(\pi) = 0$ are which numbers?

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $L = \pi$, $(n\pi/\pi)^2 = n^2$.
The formula squares the index. What is $(n\pi/\pi)^2$?
The factors of $\pi$ cancel when $L = \pi$. What is left after cancelling?
There is no factor of $2$ here. What does $n^2$ give for the eigenvalues?

vGwyyXWGR3Y.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$, what do $\lambda = 0$ and $\lambda < 0$ produce?
vGwyyXWGR3Y.hint: Check whether linear or exponential solutions can vanish at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. For $\lambda \le 0$ the solutions are lines or hyperbolic functions, which cannot vanish at both ends except trivially.
Nonzero solutions need oscillation, which requires positive $\lambda$. What do nonpositive $\lambda$ give?
Sine modes require $\lambda > 0$. What is the only solution when $\lambda \le 0$?
The solutions here are real. What real solution survives for $\lambda \le 0$?

vGwyyXWGR3Y.prompt: An eigenvalue of a boundary value problem is best defined as what?
vGwyyXWGR3Y.hint: It is the parameter value that unlocks a nonzero solution.

answerOptions.text: A value of $\lambda$ for which the problem has a nontrivial solution
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Eigenvalues are exactly the parameter values admitting a nonzero solution.

answerOptions.text: Any root of the differential equation
answerOptions.rationale: The equation has no roots in that sense; the parameter is what matters. What does an eigenvalue unlock?

answerOptions.text: The slope of the solution at the origin
answerOptions.rationale: A slope is not a parameter value. What property of $\lambda$ defines an eigenvalue?

answerOptions.text: The maximum value of the solution
answerOptions.rationale: The peak value is not the definition. What must exist for $\lambda$ to be an eigenvalue?

Elk0gjSd4c8.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ with Neumann conditions $y'(0) = 0$ and $y'(L) = 0$, the eigenfunctions are which functions?
Elk0gjSd4c8.hint: The derivative must vanish at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Cosines have zero slope at $x = 0$ and $x = L$, and $n = 0$ gives the constant mode.
Sine has nonzero slope at $x = 0$. Which function has zero derivative there?
Exponentials do not meet zero-slope conditions at both ends. What oscillatory function does?
A bare factor of $x$ spoils the slope condition at $0$. What pure cosine works?

Elk0gjSd4c8.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(\pi) = 0$, what is the smallest eigenvalue and its eigenfunction?

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The lowest mode is $n = 1$, giving $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\sin x$.
The constant function cannot vanish at the ends. What is the lowest sine mode?
With $L = \pi$ the eigenvalues are $n^2$. What does $n = 1$ give?
That is the $n$ such that $n^2 = 4$ would not give $2$ either; check $n = 1$. What is $\lambda_1$?

Elk0gjSd4c8.prompt: How many eigenvalues does a regular two-point eigenvalue problem have?
Elk0gjSd4c8.hint: Think about whether the modes ever stop.

answerOptions.text: Infinitely many, forming an increasing sequence
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The eigenvalues form an infinite sequence increasing without bound.
There is no fixed small count; the modes continue indefinitely. How many are there?
One mode is far too few; higher harmonics exist. How many eigenvalues appear?
The length scales the eigenvalues but does not cap their number. How many are there in total?

Elk0gjSd4c8.prompt: As the mode number $n$ increases, the eigenvalues $\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2$ do what?

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Since $\lambda_n$ grows like $n^2$, the eigenvalues tend to infinity.
There is no ceiling on $n^2$. What happens to $\lambda_n$ as $n$ grows?
Larger $n$ makes $(n\pi/L)^2$ larger, not smaller. Which direction do they move?
The sequence is monotonic in $n$. What single trend does $n^2$ follow?

Elk0gjSd4c8.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(2) = 0$, the eigenvalues are which?

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $L = 2$, the eigenvalues are $(n\pi/2)^2$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$
The length $L = 2$ divides inside the square. Where does the $2$ go?
You must square the quantity $n\pi/2$. What is its square?
That corresponds to $L = \pi$, not $L = 2$. How does $L = 2$ change the formula?

qIfxydBEdzg.prompt: The core idea of the shooting method is to convert a boundary value problem into what?
qIfxydBEdzg.hint: It replaces a far-end condition with a guess at the start.

answerOptions.text: An initial value problem with a guessed initial slope
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The method guesses the missing initial slope and integrates forward as an IVP.

answerOptions.text: A system of algebraic equations only
answerOptions.rationale: Integration of an IVP is still required. What kind of problem does shooting solve repeatedly?
No new independent variable is introduced. What single-point problem replaces the BVP?
The method computes numerically, not by sketching. What initial-value form does it use?

qIfxydBEdzg.prompt: In the shooting method, what is adjusted until the far boundary condition is met?
qIfxydBEdzg.hint: You vary the quantity you had to guess at the start.

answerOptions.text: The guessed initial slope (or missing initial value)
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. You tune the guessed initial slope until the solution hits the target at the far end.
The equation stays fixed; only the guess changes. What unknown starting quantity is tuned?
The interval is given by the problem. What free starting value do you adjust?
The order is fixed. Which guessed initial datum gets varied?

qIfxydBEdzg.prompt: Finding the correct initial slope amounts to solving which kind of problem?
qIfxydBEdzg.hint: You want the far-end mismatch to be zero.

answerOptions.text: A root-finding problem for the boundary mismatch as a function of the guess
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. You seek the guess that makes the endpoint error zero, a root-finding problem.
You want zero error, not a maximum. What value of the mismatch are you seeking?
No integration by parts is involved. What condition on the mismatch must hold?
Diagonalization is not the tool here. What numerical goal does the slope satisfy?

qIfxydBEdzg.prompt: For a linear boundary value problem, how many shots are needed to find the exact slope by interpolation?
qIfxydBEdzg.hint: Linear dependence on the guess means a straight-line fit.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The endpoint value depends linearly on the guess, so two shots determine the exact slope by interpolation.
One shot gives one data point, not enough to interpolate. How many are needed for a line?
Linearity makes the process finite. How many points define the needed line?
There is no fixed count like ten; linearity needs only enough for a line. How many is that?

qIfxydBEdzg.prompt: Each shot in the shooting method is carried out using what?
qIfxydBEdzg.hint: Forward integration of an initial value problem needs a numerical integrator.

answerOptions.text: A standard initial value problem solver such as Runge-Kutta
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each shot integrates the IVP numerically, typically with a Runge-Kutta method.
The steps are numerical integrations, not symbolic factoring. What solver advances each shot?
Fourier methods are not used for a single shot. What numerical integrator is used?
No determinant is computed per shot. What kind of solver integrates the IVP?

tMO28AakkZ8.prompt: If $y(b; s)$ is the computed endpoint value for guessed slope $s$ and the target is $\beta$, what equation does shooting solve?
tMO28AakkZ8.hint: You want the computed endpoint to match the target.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The mismatch $F(s) = y(b; s) - \beta$ is driven to zero to satisfy the far boundary condition.
The condition is a match, so you subtract the target. What sign relates $y(b; s)$ and $\beta$?
The guess $s$ is a slope, not directly the endpoint value. What computed quantity must equal $\beta$?
A product is not the condition. What difference should vanish?

tMO28AakkZ8.prompt: For a nonlinear boundary value problem, the root $F(s) = 0$ is typically found using what?
tMO28AakkZ8.hint: Nonlinear mismatch functions call for iterative root finders.

answerOptions.text: An iterative method such as Newton's method or bisection
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Nonlinear mismatch functions require iterative root finding like Newton or bisection.
Linear interpolation is exact only for linear problems. What is needed when $F$ is nonlinear?
A nonlinear root rarely yields to one substitution. What iterative tool is used?
That technique applies to certain ODEs, not to root finding. What numerical iteration solves $F(s) = 0$?

tMO28AakkZ8.prompt: A practical advantage of the shooting method is that it lets you reuse what?
tMO28AakkZ8.hint: It leans on machinery already built for one-point problems.

answerOptions.text: Robust, well-developed initial value problem integrators
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Shooting reduces a BVP to repeated IVPs, reusing mature initial value solvers.
Closed forms are usually unavailable; the method is numerical. What existing solvers does it reuse?
No Fourier table is required. What kind of solver does shooting build on?

answerOptions.text: Matrix inversion of the boundary data
answerOptions.rationale: Direct inversion is a different approach. What IVP machinery does shooting exploit?

tMO28AakkZ8.prompt: How do boundary value problems differ from initial value problems in where conditions are imposed?
tMO28AakkZ8.hint: Compare a single starting point with two separated points.

answerOptions.text: BVP conditions are split across two endpoints; IVP conditions are all at one point
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. That split is exactly what forces the indirect shooting approach.

answerOptions.text: BVP and IVP conditions are imposed identically
answerOptions.rationale: If they were the same, no special method would be needed. How are they placed differently?

answerOptions.text: BVP conditions are imposed at infinitely many points
answerOptions.rationale: Two endpoints carry the conditions, not infinitely many. How are they distributed?

answerOptions.text: IVP conditions are imposed at two endpoints
answerOptions.rationale: That describes a BVP. Where are IVP conditions actually placed?

tMO28AakkZ8.prompt: Why can a single forward integration not directly solve a two-point boundary value problem?
tMO28AakkZ8.hint: One end is missing a needed starting datum.

answerOptions.text: The initial data needed to start the integration is incomplete at one end
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. One endpoint lacks the slope (or value) needed to begin, so the missing datum must be guessed.
Instability is not the central issue here. What starting information is missing?
The problem can be perfectly solvable. What prevents a direct single integration?

answerOptions.text: Boundary value problems are not differential equations
answerOptions.rationale: They certainly are differential equations. What initial datum is unavailable at one end?

J3S4lBADQVw.prompt: The inner product of two functions $f$ and $g$ on $[a, b]$ is defined as which expression?
J3S4lBADQVw.hint: It generalizes the dot product to functions by integrating their product.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The function inner product integrates the product of $f$ and $g$ over the interval.
That is a boundary difference, not an integral. What operation over $[a,b]$ defines the inner product?
You multiply the functions, not add them. What integrand appears in the inner product?
A pointwise quotient is not an inner product. What integral of a product is used?

J3S4lBADQVw.prompt: Two functions are orthogonal on $[a, b]$ when which quantity equals zero?
J3S4lBADQVw.hint: Orthogonality is the function analog of perpendicular vectors.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Orthogonality means the inner product, the integral of the product, vanishes.
A zero sum would make them negatives, not orthogonal. What integral must vanish?
Endpoint differences are unrelated to orthogonality. What integral equals zero?
The product need not vanish pointwise, only its integral. What integral is zero?

J3S4lBADQVw.hint: Distinct sine harmonics are mutually orthogonal on this interval.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Different sine harmonics are orthogonal on $[-\pi, \pi]$, so the integral is zero.
A nonzero value occurs only when $m = n$. What is the integral when $m \neq n$?
That is closer to a normalization for $m = n$ in a different convention. What does orthogonality give for $m \neq n$?
The integral is not normalized to one here. What value reflects orthogonality?

J3S4lBADQVw.prompt: What is $\int_0^L \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right)dx$ when $m = n$?
J3S4lBADQVw.hint: The same harmonic against itself gives the squared norm.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The integral of $\sin^2(n\pi x/L)$ over $[0, L]$ equals $L/2$.
A zero value happens only when $m \neq n$. What is the self inner product when $m = n$?
The average of $\sin^2$ is $1/2$, so the integral is $L/2$, not $L$. What halves it?
The mean value of $\sin^2$ over a full set of modes is $1/2$. What does that give over length $L$?

J3S4lBADQVw.prompt: The norm of a function $f$ with respect to the inner product is defined as what?
J3S4lBADQVw.hint: It is the function analog of vector length.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The norm is the square root of the inner product of $f$ with itself.
That is the plain integral, not the norm. What square root of a self inner product is used?
The peak value is a different norm; here we use the integral form. What square root defines it?
An endpoint difference is unrelated to length. What integral expression gives the norm?

ZYf0tz9oVz8.prompt: Why is orthogonality so useful for expanding a function in a series of basis functions?
ZYf0tz9oVz8.hint: Orthogonality decouples one coefficient from all the others.

answerOptions.text: It lets each coefficient be computed independently by a single inner product
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Orthogonality isolates each coefficient through one inner product, with no coupling between terms.

answerOptions.text: It makes the basis functions identical
answerOptions.rationale: Orthogonal functions are distinct, not identical. What does orthogonality let you compute term by term?

answerOptions.text: It removes the need for any integration
answerOptions.rationale: Coefficients still come from integrals; orthogonality just decouples them. What does it isolate?

answerOptions.text: It guarantees a finite number of terms
answerOptions.rationale: Series can still be infinite. What computational simplification does orthogonality provide?

ZYf0tz9oVz8.hint: Distinct cosine harmonics are orthogonal on this interval.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Different cosine harmonics are orthogonal on $[-\pi, \pi]$.
A nonzero value arises only for $m = n$. What is the integral when $m \neq n$?
That would relate to the $m = n = 0$ case. What does orthogonality give for distinct nonzero harmonics?
Orthogonality gives a clean zero, not a negative value. What is the integral for $m \neq n$?

ZYf0tz9oVz8.prompt: For all integers $m$ and $n$, what is $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \sin(mx)\cos(nx)\,dx$?
ZYf0tz9oVz8.hint: Sines are odd and cosines are even; consider the parity of the product.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The product of a sine and a cosine integrates to zero over the symmetric interval, so sines and cosines are mutually orthogonal.
Sine against cosine never matches the way sine against sine does. What is the integral for every $m$ and $n$?
The integral is not normalized to one. What value reflects sine-cosine orthogonality?
The integral is perfectly well defined. What value does the odd integrand give?

ZYf0tz9oVz8.prompt: The set $\{1, \cos x, \sin x, \cos 2x, \sin 2x, \dots\}$ has which property on $[-\pi, \pi]$?
ZYf0tz9oVz8.hint: Every distinct pair integrates to zero against each other.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Every distinct pair from this set is orthogonal on $[-\pi, \pi]$, which underlies Fourier series.
These functions are independent. What stronger relation does pairwise zero inner product give?
Distinct pairs integrate to zero, not one. What name describes that?
The set reaches far beyond constants. What orthogonality property does it have?

ZYf0tz9oVz8.prompt: An orthonormal set of functions is an orthogonal set with which extra property?
ZYf0tz9oVz8.hint: Normalize each function to unit length.

answerOptions.text: Each function has norm equal to one
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Orthonormal means orthogonal plus every function having unit norm.
Many useful functions do not integrate to zero. What normalization makes a set orthonormal?
Orthonormal functions are distinct. What unit property is added to orthogonality?
Orthonormality is not about being polynomial. What norm condition is required?

Oz_cnaz9zSo.prompt: A Sturm-Liouville equation is written in which standard form?
Oz_cnaz9zSo.hint: It packages the derivative terms into a single divergence-style expression.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The self-adjoint Sturm-Liouville form collects the second-order term as $(p y')'$ with a weight $w$ multiplying $\lambda$.
That is one special case, not the general form. What general structure with $p$, $q$, and $w$ is used?
The leading term must be the derivative of $p y'$, not $p y''$. What self-adjoint form is standard?
A first-order form does not capture Sturm-Liouville problems. What second-order self-adjoint form is correct?

Oz_cnaz9zSo.prompt: A regular Sturm-Liouville problem is posed on a finite interval with which kind of boundary conditions?
Oz_cnaz9zSo.hint: The conditions act separately at each endpoint.

answerOptions.text: Separated boundary conditions at each endpoint
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A regular problem uses separated conditions, one at each end, with $p$ and $w$ positive on the interval.
Boundary conditions are essential to the eigenvalue problem. What separated conditions are imposed?

answerOptions.text: Conditions only at the left endpoint
answerOptions.rationale: Both ends must be constrained. What kind of conditions does a regular problem use?
Periodic conditions define a different (periodic) Sturm-Liouville problem. What does the regular case use?

Oz_cnaz9zSo.prompt: For a regular Sturm-Liouville problem, the eigenvalues are guaranteed to be what?
Oz_cnaz9zSo.hint: A key theorem rules out complex eigenvalues.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The self-adjoint structure guarantees that all eigenvalues are real.
The self-adjoint form forbids genuinely complex eigenvalues. What kind must they be?
Eigenvalues are not forced to be negative. What property is actually guaranteed?
There is no such normalization. What does the theory guarantee about the eigenvalues?

Oz_cnaz9zSo.prompt: Eigenfunctions of a Sturm-Liouville problem belonging to distinct eigenvalues are what?
Oz_cnaz9zSo.hint: They satisfy a weighted orthogonality relation.

answerOptions.text: Orthogonal with respect to the weight $w(x)$
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Distinct-eigenvalue eigenfunctions are orthogonal under the weighted inner product with weight $w$.
Distinct eigenvalues give distinct eigenfunctions. What relation holds between them?
They are not multiples of one another. What weighted relation do they satisfy?
The orthogonality uses the weight $w$, not the unweighted inner product. What weight appears?

Oz_cnaz9zSo.prompt: The eigenvalues of a regular Sturm-Liouville problem form which kind of sequence?
Oz_cnaz9zSo.hint: They line up in order and never stop increasing.

answerOptions.text: An increasing sequence tending to infinity
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The eigenvalues are real, simple, ordered, and grow without bound.
The eigenvalues do not stop after finitely many. How many are there, and how are they ordered?
They increase rather than decrease. Which direction does the sequence go?
They follow a strict increasing order. What pattern does the sequence have?

l9Dc1ab9RaU.prompt: Writing $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ in Sturm-Liouville form $(p y')' + (q + \lambda w)y = 0$ uses which functions?
l9Dc1ab9RaU.hint: Match the plain second derivative to $(p y')'$ with no extra factors.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $p = 1$ the term $(p y')' = y''$, and $q = 0$, $w = 1$ recover $y'' + \lambda y = 0$.
If $p = 0$ the second-derivative term disappears. What value of $p$ gives $(p y')' = y''$?
A nonconstant $p$ would introduce a $y'$ term. What constant $p$ reproduces $y''$?
With $w = 0$ the eigenvalue term vanishes. Which function should multiply $\lambda$?

l9Dc1ab9RaU.prompt: In a Sturm-Liouville problem, the weight function $w(x)$ does what?
l9Dc1ab9RaU.hint: It appears inside the inner product that defines orthogonality.

answerOptions.text: Defines the weighted inner product under which eigenfunctions are orthogonal
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The weight $w$ sets the inner product $\int f g\, w\, dx$ that makes the eigenfunctions orthogonal.
That role belongs to $p$, not $w$. Where does the weight $w$ appear?
Boundary conditions are imposed separately. What integral structure does $w$ define?

answerOptions.text: Has no effect on the problem
answerOptions.rationale: The weight is central to orthogonality. What inner product does it determine?

l9Dc1ab9RaU.prompt: The orthogonality of Sturm-Liouville eigenfunctions ultimately follows from which structural property?
l9Dc1ab9RaU.hint: The operator equals its own adjoint.

answerOptions.text: The self-adjoint (symmetric) form of the operator
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Writing the operator in self-adjoint form is exactly what forces real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenfunctions.
Sturm-Liouville problems are linear. What symmetry of the operator drives orthogonality?
Boundary conditions are required and help establish self-adjointness. What operator property is key?

answerOptions.text: The presence of a forcing term
answerOptions.rationale: These eigenvalue problems are homogeneous. What structural property guarantees orthogonality?

l9Dc1ab9RaU.prompt: A central payoff of Sturm-Liouville theory is that the eigenfunctions form what?
l9Dc1ab9RaU.hint: Any reasonable function can be expanded in them.

answerOptions.text: A complete basis for expanding functions in eigenfunction series
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The eigenfunctions are complete, so general functions can be expanded in eigenfunction (generalized Fourier) series.

answerOptions.text: A finite set spanning only polynomials
answerOptions.rationale: The set is infinite and far richer than polynomials. What expansion property do they provide?

answerOptions.text: A basis only for the constant functions
answerOptions.rationale: They represent much more than constants. What completeness do they have?

answerOptions.text: A set that cannot represent any function
answerOptions.rationale: They can represent broad classes of functions. What basis property do they possess?

l9Dc1ab9RaU.prompt: How does the Dirichlet eigenvalue problem $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ relate to Sturm-Liouville theory?
l9Dc1ab9RaU.hint: Recognize it as a particular choice of $p$, $q$, and $w$.

answerOptions.text: It is a special case of a regular Sturm-Liouville problem
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. It is the Sturm-Liouville problem with $p = 1$, $q = 0$, $w = 1$ and separated conditions.

answerOptions.text: It is unrelated to Sturm-Liouville theory
answerOptions.rationale: It fits the framework exactly. Which choice of $p$, $q$, $w$ makes it Sturm-Liouville?
The problem is linear and self-adjoint. How does it sit inside the theory?
The equation is second order. What kind of Sturm-Liouville case is it?

unitMastery.prompt: What distinguishes a boundary value problem from an initial value problem?
unitMastery.hint: Consider where the side conditions are imposed.

answerOptions.text: Conditions are imposed at two different points rather than all at one point
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. A BVP spreads its conditions across two endpoints, unlike the single-point IVP.
Linearity is not the distinction; both kinds can be linear. Where are the conditions placed?
Order is unrelated to this distinction. What about the placement of conditions?
A BVP has conditions, just at two ends. How are they distributed compared with an IVP?

unitMastery.prompt: A second-order differential equation requires how many side conditions to pin down a particular solution?
unitMastery.hint: Match the count to the number of arbitrary constants.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Two arbitrary constants require two conditions.
One leaves a constant unfixed. How many constants does second order carry?
That exceeds the number of constants. How many does second order need?
Without conditions the solution stays general. How many are required?

unitMastery.prompt: Regarding existence and uniqueness, boundary value problems differ from initial value problems in what way?
unitMastery.hint: Recall the clean IVP guarantee and whether two endpoints preserve it.

answerOptions.text: A BVP may have no solution, one solution, or infinitely many
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The two-endpoint structure breaks the IVP uniqueness guarantee.

answerOptions.text: A BVP always has a unique solution
answerOptions.rationale: That clean guarantee is exactly what fails. What outcomes are possible at two endpoints?
Many do have solutions. What range of outcomes is possible?
The structures differ in outcomes. How does the BVP differ?

unitMastery.prompt: A Dirichlet boundary condition prescribes which quantity, and a Neumann condition prescribes which?
unitMastery.hint: One fixes the value, the other fixes the slope.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Dirichlet sets the function value and Neumann sets the derivative at the endpoint.
The roles are reversed here. Which condition names the value itself?
Second derivatives are not what these conditions set. Which derivatives or values do they name?
These are pointwise conditions, not integrals. What does each prescribe at the endpoint?

unitMastery.prompt: Steady-state heat conduction in a rod with fixed end temperatures is modeled as which type of problem?
unitMastery.hint: Conditions sit at the two ends in space.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Fixing temperatures at both ends imposes conditions at two points in space.
The conditions are spatial endpoints, not a single starting time. What type is that?
Two fixed end temperatures are real conditions. Where do they sit?
The model is a differential equation. What kind of conditions does it carry?

unitMastery.prompt: Solve $y'' = 0$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $y(2) = 5$.
unitMastery.hint: The general solution is a line; fit both points.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. From $y = c_1 + c_2 x$, $y(0) = 1$ gives $c_1 = 1$, and $y(2) = 1 + 2c_2 = 5$ gives $c_2 = 2$.
Check $y(2)$: it must equal $5$, not $11$. What slope makes $y(2) = 5$?
This violates $y(0) = 1$. What constant term satisfies the left endpoint?
The solution of $y'' = 0$ is linear, not quadratic. What line fits both points?

unitMastery.prompt: What is the standard method for a linear two-point boundary value problem?
unitMastery.hint: Build the general solution first.

answerOptions.text: Find the general solution, then impose both boundary conditions
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The constants are fixed by applying the two endpoint conditions to the general solution.
The general solution must come first. What is the proper order?
Both endpoints must be satisfied. What systematic approach uses the general solution?
The conditions are applied as given. What do you do with the general solution?

unitMastery.prompt: The problem $y'' + y = 0$ with $y(0) = 0$, $y(\pi) = 0$ has how many solutions?
unitMastery.hint: After $A = 0$, test whether the second condition restricts $B$.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. $y(0) = 0$ forces $A = 0$, and $\sin\pi = 0$ leaves $B$ free.
The second condition holds for any $B$. What family of nonzero solutions remains?
At least the zero solution exists, and more. What does $\sin\pi = 0$ allow?
The constant $B$ is unrestricted. How many values can it take?

unitMastery.prompt: A homogeneous boundary value problem $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ always admits which solution?
unitMastery.hint: Consider the identically zero function.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The zero function satisfies the equation and both conditions for every $\lambda$.
Nonzero solutions appear only at eigenvalues. What always works?
The zero function always solves it. What is that solution?
A nonzero constant cannot vanish at the ends. What trivial solution fits?

unitMastery.prompt: Solve $y'' = 2$ with $y(0) = 0$ and $y(1) = 0$.
unitMastery.hint: Integrate twice to get $y = x^2 + c_1 x + c_2$, then apply the endpoints.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. $y = x^2 + c_1 x + c_2$ with $y(0) = 0$ gives $c_2 = 0$, and $y(1) = 1 + c_1 = 0$ gives $c_1 = -1$.
This gives $y(1) = 1 \neq 0$. What linear term makes $y(1) = 0$?
Then $y(1) = 2$, not $0$. What sign should the linear coefficient have?
That solves $y'' = 0$, not $y'' = 2$. What quadratic results from integrating twice?

unitMastery.prompt: For $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$, the eigenvalues are which?
unitMastery.hint: Sine must vanish at $x = L$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Requiring $\sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\,L) = 0$ gives $\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2$.
You must square $\sqrt{\lambda}\,L = n\pi$. What is $\lambda$ itself?
Negative values give only the trivial solution. What sign gives oscillation?
The length $L$ must appear. How does $L$ enter the formula?

unitMastery.prompt: The eigenfunctions of $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ are which?
unitMastery.hint: They vanish at both ends.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. These sines are zero at $x = 0$ and $x = L$.
Cosine is nonzero at $x = 0$. Which function vanishes at both ends?
Exponentials cannot meet zero conditions at both ends. What oscillatory function does?
Powers do not vanish at $x = L$ in general. What sine fits?

unitMastery.prompt: For $L = \pi$, the eigenvalues of $y'' + \lambda y = 0$, $y(0) = y(\pi) = 0$ are which numbers?
unitMastery.hint: Set $L = \pi$ in $(n\pi/L)^2$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $L = \pi$ the factors of $\pi$ cancel, leaving $n^2$.
The formula squares the index. What is $(n\pi/\pi)^2$?
The $\pi$ cancels when $L = \pi$. What remains?
There is no factor of $2$. What does $n^2$ give?

unitMastery.prompt: For Neumann conditions $y'(0) = y'(L) = 0$, the eigenfunctions of $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ are which?
unitMastery.hint: The derivative must vanish at both ends, and a constant mode is allowed.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. Cosines have zero slope at both ends, and $n = 0$ gives the constant mode.
Sine has nonzero slope at $x = 0$. Which function has zero derivative there?
Powers do not satisfy zero-slope conditions. What pure cosine works?
Exponentials fail the slope conditions. What oscillatory function fits?

unitMastery.prompt: As the mode number $n$ grows, the eigenvalues $\lambda_n = (n\pi/L)^2$ behave how?
unitMastery.hint: Track the growth of $n^2$.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Growing like $n^2$, the eigenvalues tend to infinity.
There is no ceiling on $n^2$. Which direction do they go?
Larger $n$ makes them larger. Which way do they move?
They are all positive here. What single trend do they follow?

unitMastery.prompt: The shooting method converts a boundary value problem into what?
unitMastery.hint: It guesses the missing starting datum.

answerOptions.text: An initial value problem with a guessed initial slope
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. It guesses the missing initial slope and integrates as an IVP.
No new variable is introduced. What single-point problem replaces the BVP?
Integration is still required. What initial-value problem does it solve?
Shooting uses IVP integration, not Fourier expansion. What problem form does it create?

unitMastery.prompt: In the shooting method, the guessed initial slope is adjusted until what holds?
unitMastery.hint: You want the far-end condition met.

answerOptions.text: The solution matches the far boundary condition
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The slope is tuned until the integrated solution hits the target at the far end.
A constant solution is not the goal. What far-end requirement is enforced?
The equation is fixed. What endpoint condition must be satisfied?
The interval is given. What boundary value is matched?

unitMastery.prompt: For a linear boundary value problem, how many shots determine the exact initial slope?
unitMastery.hint: Linear dependence on the guess means a straight-line fit.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The endpoint depends linearly on the guess, so two shots fix it exactly.
One point is not enough to interpolate a line. How many points are needed?
Linearity makes it finite. How many define the line?

answerOptions.text: It cannot be found by shooting
answerOptions.rationale: Linear problems are the easiest case for shooting. How many shots suffice?

unitMastery.prompt: With $F(s) = y(b; s) - \beta$ the boundary mismatch, the shooting method seeks what?
unitMastery.hint: You want the mismatch to vanish.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The guess that makes the endpoint error zero solves the problem.
You want zero error, not a maximum. What value of $F$ is sought?
You drive $F$ itself to zero, not its derivative. What condition is the goal?
The mismatch should vanish, not merely stay constant. What root is sought?

unitMastery.prompt: A key practical benefit of the shooting method is that it lets you reuse what?
unitMastery.hint: It builds on well-tested one-point machinery.

answerOptions.text: Robust initial value problem integrators such as Runge-Kutta
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Each shot is an IVP integration, reusing mature solvers.
Closed forms are usually unavailable. What numerical machinery is reused?
No Fourier table is needed. What solvers does shooting rely on?
Eigenvalue routines are a different approach. What IVP tools does shooting reuse?

unitMastery.prompt: The inner product of functions $f$ and $g$ on $[a, b]$ is which expression?
unitMastery.hint: It integrates the product of the two functions.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The function inner product integrates the product over the interval.
That is a boundary difference. What integral defines the inner product?
You multiply, not add. What integrand appears?
A pointwise quotient is not an inner product. What integral of a product is used?

unitMastery.prompt: Two functions are orthogonal on $[a, b]$ when which quantity is zero?
unitMastery.hint: It is the function analog of perpendicular vectors.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Orthogonality means the integral of the product vanishes.
A zero sum makes them negatives, not orthogonal. What integral vanishes?
The product need not vanish pointwise, only its integral. What integral is zero?
Endpoint values are unrelated. What integral must equal zero?

unitMastery.prompt: For $m \neq n$, what is $\int_0^L \sin(n\pi x/L)\sin(m\pi x/L)\,dx$, and what is it for $m = n$?
unitMastery.hint: Distinct modes are orthogonal; a mode against itself gives the squared norm.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. The sine modes are orthogonal, with self inner product $L/2$.
The $m = n$ case is nonzero. What is the self inner product?
Distinct modes give zero. What does orthogonality say for $m \neq n$?
The cases are reversed. Which case gives zero?

unitMastery.prompt: The set $\{1, \cos x, \sin x, \cos 2x, \sin 2x, \dots\}$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$ has which property?
unitMastery.hint: Every distinct pair integrates to zero against each other.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Every distinct pair is orthogonal on $[-\pi, \pi]$, the foundation of Fourier series.
These functions are independent. What stronger relation holds?
Distinct pairs integrate to zero, not one. What is that called?
The set reaches far beyond constants. What orthogonality property holds?

unitMastery.prompt: Why does orthogonality make computing series coefficients easy?
unitMastery.hint: Integrating against one basis function isolates its coefficient.

answerOptions.text: Each coefficient is found independently by one inner product
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Orthogonality decouples the coefficients, each from a single inner product.

answerOptions.text: It makes all the basis functions equal
answerOptions.rationale: They remain distinct. What does orthogonality let you compute term by term?
Coefficients still come from integrals. What does orthogonality decouple?

answerOptions.text: It forces a finite number of terms
answerOptions.rationale: Series may be infinite. What computational simplification does it give?

unitMastery.prompt: The standard Sturm-Liouville form of a second-order equation is which?
unitMastery.hint: It collects the leading term as a single derivative of $p y'$.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The self-adjoint form uses $(p y')'$ with a weight $w$ multiplying $\lambda$.
The leading term must be $(p y')'$, not $p y''$. What self-adjoint form is standard?
A first-order form misses the structure. What second-order form is used?
That is a narrow special case. What general $p$, $q$, $w$ form is standard?

unitMastery.prompt: For a regular Sturm-Liouville problem, the eigenvalues are guaranteed to be what?
unitMastery.hint: The self-adjoint structure rules out complex values.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. Self-adjointness forces all eigenvalues to be real.
The structure forbids genuinely complex eigenvalues. What must they be?
They are generally nonzero and varied. What property is guaranteed?
They need not be negative. What is guaranteed about them?

unitMastery.prompt: Eigenfunctions of a Sturm-Liouville problem for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal with respect to what?
unitMastery.hint: A weight function enters the inner product.

answerOptions.rationale: Yes. They are orthogonal under the weighted inner product with weight $w$.
The orthogonality is weighted by $w$. Which function provides the weight?
The weight in the inner product is $w$, not $p$. Which function weights the orthogonality?
The eigenvalue is not the weight. What function $w$ defines the inner product?

unitMastery.prompt: Writing $y'' + \lambda y = 0$ in Sturm-Liouville form uses which functions?
unitMastery.hint: Match $(p y')'$ to a plain $y''$.

answerOptions.rationale: Correct. With $p = 1$ the term $(p y')' = y''$, and $q = 0$, $w = 1$ recover the equation.
If $p = 0$ the second-derivative term disappears. What $p$ gives $y''$?
A nonconstant $p$ adds a $y'$ term. What constant $p$ works?
With $w = 0$ the eigenvalue term vanishes. Which function multiplies $\lambda$?

unitMastery.prompt: A central payoff of Sturm-Liouville theory is that its eigenfunctions form what?
unitMastery.hint: They can represent broad classes of functions.

answerOptions.text: A complete basis for eigenfunction (generalized Fourier) expansions
answerOptions.rationale: Yes. The eigenfunctions are complete, enabling expansions of general functions.

answerOptions.text: A finite set spanning only polynomials
answerOptions.rationale: The set is infinite and richer than polynomials. What expansion power do they have?
They represent far more than constants. What completeness do they possess?

answerOptions.text: A set that represents no functions
answerOptions.rationale: They represent broad function classes. What basis property do they have?
